text: Fatigue is a very common side effect of concurrent chemoradiation therapy and typically develops within the first few weeks of treatment, so what you're experiencing is expected. This is your body's normal response to the intensive cancer treatment you're receiving. You can manage this at home by prioritizing rest, maintaining adequate nutrition and hydration, and pacing your activities throughout the day rather than pushing through fatigue. However, since you're only one week past your last oncology visit, please contact your cancer team within the next 1-2 days to report this symptom—they may offer additional supportive strategies and will want to ensure there are no other contributing factors. If you develop severe weakness, shortness of breath, chest pain, or difficulty with your medications, seek immediate medical attention.

Severe diarrhea lasting three days during FOLFIRI chemotherapy requires prompt medical attention and should not be managed at home alone. FOLFIRI commonly causes diarrhea, which can lead to dehydration and electrolyte imbalances—particularly concerning given your diabetes and cardiac history. Please contact your oncology team today or visit an urgent care/emergency department if you cannot reach them, as you may need IV fluids, anti-diarrheal medications (such as loperamide), and possibly a dose adjustment to your chemotherapy. In the meantime, drink clear fluids frequently in small amounts and avoid solid foods, but do not delay seeking medical evaluation.

Do not wait for your next scheduled appointment to report these symptoms.

Mark, persistent lower back pain in your situation requires prompt evaluation by your oncology team rather than home management alone. While treatment side effects can cause musculoskeletal pain, back pain in multiple myeloma can also reflect disease progression, spinal involvement, or complications like vertebral fractures—all of which need imaging and assessment. Please contact your oncologist today or visit urgent care if you experience severe pain, numbness, weakness, or bowel/bladder changes, as these could indicate spinal cord compression requiring emergency evaluation. Your oncology team will likely want to examine you and may order imaging (such as an MRI or CT) to determine the cause and adjust your management accordingly.

Lower back pain in multiple myeloma patients requires careful evaluation, as it can result from treatment side effects, but more importantly, it may indicate disease progression or spinal involvement, which is common in myeloma. Given that you're on active chemotherapy and this is a new symptom developing a week ago, **you should contact your oncology team promptly for an in-person evaluation rather than managing this at home**. They will likely need to perform imaging (such as spine MRI or CT) to rule out spinal lesions, cord compression, or other serious complications. In the meantime, avoid heavy lifting and use over-the-counter pain relief cautiously (discuss with your oncologist first given your current medications). Do not delay—report this symptom to your cancer center as soon as possible, ideally within the next few days.

Lower back pain in a multiple myeloma patient requires prompt evaluation, as it can represent either a treatment side effect or a serious complication such as spinal involvement, pathologic fracture, or disease progression. Please contact your oncology team today to schedule an urgent assessment.

Your lower back pain warrants prompt evaluation by your oncology team, as it could represent several important issues that need to be distinguished: treatment side effects, myeloma-related bone disease (including potential pathologic fracture or spinal involvement), or an unrelated musculoskeletal problem. A rash during immunotherapy with nivolumab/ipilimumab can be a known side effect, but it requires prompt evaluation to determine severity and rule out other causes. **Please contact your oncology team today or visit an urgent care clinic to have the rash assessed in person**—do not wait for your next scheduled appointment. Your oncology team needs to examine it to distinguish between a manageable treatment-related rash (which may respond to topical steroids) versus a more serious immune-related adverse event that could require treatment adjustment or discontinuation. In the meantime, avoid scratching, use gentle skin care, and note any associated symptoms like fever, itching severity, or spread. Bring photos of the rash to your appointment if possible to help document its progression.

Depending on the rash characteristics, your oncology team may recommend in-person evaluation, skin photography, or dermatology referral to confirm the diagnosis and determine if treatment adjustment is needed. In the meantime, avoid known irritants and keep the area clean and dry.
